medications.indication_rationale: A topical imidazole is first-line for uncomplicated vulvovaginal candidiasis and is pregnancy-safe, making it the appropriate default when pregnancy status is unconfirmed remotely. An intravaginal clotrimazole pessary/cream course treats the vaginal infection; clotrimazole 1% cream to the vulva relieves the external itch and burning (as given in the source note). Available over the counter, so it can be started via a pharmacist.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Clotrimazole 1% cream applied to the vulva twice daily for up to 7 days for symptomatic relief (per source note), plus an intravaginal clotrimazole course (e.g., pessary/cream) as the standard antifungal route

medications.indication_rationale: A single oral dose of fluconazole 150 mg is an effective and convenient alternative to topical treatment for uncomplicated candidiasis (given in the source note). It must only be recommended once pregnancy has been excluded, since oral azoles are avoided in pregnancy — hence topical treatment is the safer default in this unconfirmed-pregnancy remote encounter.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Fluconazole 150 mg orally, single stat dose (per source note)
contraindications_in_this_case: Pregnancy (confirmed or possible) — avoid the oral azole and use a topical imidazole instead; pregnancy is unconfirmed in this encounter so oral fluconazole is gated on excluding pregnancy first

medications.drug_name: oral or topical antibiotics (e.g., amoxicillin, metronidazole given reflexively)
medications.indication_rationale: Antibiotics do not treat a fungal infection and would worsen it — recent broad-spectrum antibiotics are the likely precipitant of this episode by disrupting protective lactobacilli. Prescribing an antibiotic here is an error of commission. (Metronidazole is the correct treatment only if bacterial vaginosis or trichomoniasis is diagnosed — neither is present here.)
contraindications_in_this_case: No bacterial infection is present; antibiotics would perpetuate the candidal overgrowth

medications.indication_rationale: A topical steroid may transiently soothe itch but does not treat the infection and can worsen fungal overgrowth if used alone as the primary treatment. It is not the appropriate first-line management.
contraindications_in_this_case: Untreated fungal infection — corticosteroid monotherapy can exacerbate candidal overgrowth

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Wear loose-fitting 100% cotton underwear and avoid tight synthetic clothing; avoid scented soaps, bubble baths, and 'feminine hygiene' sprays or washes in the genital area.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces the warm, moist, chemically irritated environment that favours candidal overgrowth and lets the irritated skin recover. (The source note's claim that this alone makes yeast survival 'impossible' overstates the effect — it is a helpful adjunct, not a cure.)

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Complete the full antifungal course even after the itch settles, and avoid sexual intercourse until symptoms have fully resolved to prevent further irritation of the inflamed skin.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Ensures eradication and avoids mechanical re-irritation of raw tissue during recovery.

patient_education_points.point: The recent antibiotics reduced the normal protective bacteria and let the natural yeast overgrow — that is what is causing the clumpy discharge and the raw, burning skin. An antifungal treats it; an antibiotic would make it worse.
The itch and burning usually improve within 24-48 hours of treatment, and the discharge and redness settle over about 5-7 days.
If there is any chance you could be pregnant, tell the pharmacist or GP before taking an oral antifungal tablet — a topical cream or pessary is the safer option in pregnancy.

If this keeps coming back (four or more times a year) or does not clear with treatment, see your GP — recurrent episodes can occasionally point to an underlying cause such as diabetes and may need a longer course.
Avoid sex until it has fully cleared, to stop further irritation of the sore skin.

follow_up_plan.interval: Review with your regular GP in about 1 week if symptoms have not fully resolved (sooner if any warning sign appears)
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Resolution of itch, burning and discharge; if not resolving or atypical, arrange a vaginal swab / wet mount and reconsider bacterial vaginosis or trichomoniasis; screen for a recurrent pattern and predisposing factors
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Fever, severe pelvic or lower-abdominal pain, redness spreading beyond the groin, a new foul/fishy-smelling discharge, or painful blisters — seek review sooner

must_include_items: Recognise this as an uncomplicated vaginal thrush / candidal vulvovaginitis from the curd-like discharge, intense itch/burning, and recent antibiotics
Recommend an appropriate antifungal — a topical imidazole (pregnancy-safe default) and/or a single-dose oral fluconazole once pregnancy is excluded
Advise avoiding irritants (scented soaps/sprays) and wearing loose cotton underwear
Provide safety-netting for red flags (fever, severe pelvic pain, spreading redness, fishy-smelling discharge, blisters)
Ask about / account for pregnancy before recommending an oral azole
acceptable_alternatives: Topical clotrimazole (pessary/cream) alone is an acceptable complete treatment
Single-dose oral fluconazole 150 mg alone is acceptable once pregnancy is excluded
Directing the patient to a pharmacist for an over-the-counter antifungal, with safety-netting, is an acceptable (T0 self-care) answer
Arranging routine GP review for treatment is acceptable
errors_of_omission: Failing to recommend any antifungal treatment
Failing to safety-net for pelvic infection / STI red flags
Not addressing pregnancy status before recommending an oral azole
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging an uncomplicated presentation to ED, ambulance, or urgent in-person assessment
Recommending oral fluconazole without excluding pregnancy
Recommending a topical corticosteroid as the primary treatment
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is a low-acuity, telehealth/pharmacist-manageable case. A pass requires recommending an appropriate antifungal AND providing safety-netting. Self-care / pharmacist antifungal advice with safety-netting is a CORRECT, passing answer — over-triaging to urgent in-person care, or prescribing an antibiotic, is the failure mode this case guards against, not a pass condition.
